title: Principal Component Analysis
definition: An unsupervised dimensionality reduction technique that transforms features into a new set of orthogonal, uncorrelated components ordered by the amount of variance they explain. The first principal component explains the most variance.
why_used: Reduces dimensionality to overcome the curse of dimensionality, speeds up training, removes multicollinearity, and enables 2D/3D visualization of high-dimensional data.
interview_point: PCA finds directions of maximum variance using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). Key decision: how many components to keep — use the explained variance ratio: keep components that explain 95%+ variance. PCA is UNSUPERVISED — it ignores class labels. Use LDA if you want class-aware dimensionality reduction.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Always scale before PCA!
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)

# Find optimal n_components
pca = PCA()
pca.fit(X_scaled)
cumulative_var = pca.explained_variance_ratio_.cumsum()
n_components = (cumulative_var < 0.95).sum() + 1
print(f'Components for 95% variance: {n_components}')

# Apply PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=n_components)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)
print(f'Shape: {X_train.shape} → {X_pca.shape}')
```

title: t-SNE
definition: A non-linear dimensionality reduction technique optimized for 2D/3D visualization. Preserves local neighborhood structure — nearby points in high dimensions stay nearby in the low-dimensional embedding.
why_used: Best technique for visualizing clusters in high-dimensional data (embeddings, gene expression, images). Creates intuitive 2D maps where clusters are visually separated.
interview_point: t-SNE is NON-DETERMINISTIC (different runs give different results without random_state). Perplexity (5-50) controls how many neighbors are considered local — higher perplexity = more global structure preserved. t-SNE is ONLY for visualization — the output cannot be used as features for downstream models (not invertible). UMAP is faster and preserves more global structure.
limitations: Cannot be applied to new data (no transform)
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# t-SNE for visualization only!
tsne = TSNE(
    n_components=2,
    perplexity=30,      # Try: 5, 30, 50
    learning_rate='auto',
    max_iter=1000,
    random_state=42     # For reproducibility
)
X_2d = tsne.fit_transform(X_high_dim)  # WARNING: O(n²)

plt.scatter(X_2d[:, 0], X_2d[:, 1], c=y, cmap='tab10', alpha=0.7)
plt.title('t-SNE Visualization')
plt.colorbar()
plt.show()
```

title: UMAP
definition: A non-linear dimensionality reduction technique based on Riemannian geometry and algebraic topology. Faster than t-SNE, preserves both local AND global structure, and supports out-of-sample transformation.
why_used: Better than t-SNE for both visualization and feature engineering. Can be used as a preprocessing step (unlike t-SNE) because it supports transform() for new data. Much faster on large datasets.
interview_point: UMAP vs t-SNE: UMAP is faster O(n log n) vs O(n²), preserves global structure better, supports transform() for new points, and can reduce to > 2 dimensions for feature engineering. n_neighbors controls local vs global structure (low=local, high=global). min_dist controls cluster compactness.
limitations: Interpretation of axes is not meaningful
real_world_example:
```python
import umap
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# UMAP for visualization + feature engineering
reducer = umap.UMAP(
    n_components=2,        # 2 for viz, >2 for features
    n_neighbors=15,        # Controls local vs global
    min_dist=0.1,          # Controls cluster tightness
    metric='euclidean',
    random_state=42
)

X_2d = reducer.fit_transform(X_train)

# Unlike t-SNE, can transform new data!
X_test_2d = reducer.transform(X_test)

plt.scatter(X_2d[:, 0], X_2d[:, 1], c=y_train, cmap='Spectral', alpha=0.5)
plt.show()
```

title: Recursive Feature Elimination
definition: An iterative feature selection method that fits a model, ranks features by importance, removes the least important feature(s), and repeats until the desired number of features remains.
why_used: Automatically identifies the most relevant features by backward elimination, guided by model-specific feature importance or coefficients.
interview_point: RFE is wrapper-based — it uses the model's learning ability to select features, which is more powerful than filter methods (correlation) but more computationally expensive. RFECV adds cross-validation to find the optimal number of features automatically.
advantages: Guided by model's actual feature utility
Works with any model that has feature_importances_ or coef_
RFECV automatically finds optimal feature count
limitations: Feature ranking depends on model choice
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.feature_selection import RFECV
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

# RFECV: automatically find best number of features
estimator = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)

rfecv = RFECV(
    estimator=estimator,
    step=1,         # Remove 1 feature per iteration
    cv=5,
    scoring='roc_auc',
    n_jobs=-1
)
rfecv.fit(X_train, y_train)

print(f'Optimal features: {rfecv.n_features_}')
print(f'Selected: {X.columns[rfecv.support_].tolist()}')
X_selected = rfecv.transform(X_train)
```

title: Feature Importance
definition: A score assigned to each feature indicating how useful it is in predicting the target variable. Tree-based models compute it via impurity reduction (Gini/entropy) or permutation of features.
why_used: Enables feature selection by identifying and removing low-importance features. Also provides interpretability of which inputs drive model decisions.
interview_point: Tree-based feature importance has a BIAS toward high-cardinality features — features with many unique values get higher scores because they create more possible splits. Solution: use Permutation Importance or SHAP values, which are model-agnostic and unbiased.
advantages: Fast to compute (built into tree models)
limitations: Tree importance biased toward high-cardinality features
Measures impurity reduction, not causal importance
Correlated features may show low importance
real_world_example:
```python
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Standard feature importance
fi = pd.Series(model.feature_importances_, index=feature_names)
fi = fi.sort_values(ascending=False)
print(fi.head(10))

# Better: Permutation importance (less biased)
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance
perm_imp = permutation_importance(model, X_val, y_val, n_repeats=30, random_state=42)
for i in perm_imp.importances_mean.argsort()[::-1][:10]:
    print(f'{feature_names[i]}: {perm_imp.importances_mean[i]:.4f}')
```

title: Polynomial Features
definition: Creates new features from existing ones by computing polynomial combinations and interactions. degree=2 creates: x₁², x₂², x₁·x₂. Enables linear models to capture non-linear relationships.
why_used: Enables linear models (logistic regression, linear SVM) to capture non-linear decision boundaries without switching to a complex model.
interview_point: Polynomial features grow combinatorially: n features with degree 2 creates O(n²/2) new features. With 100 features and degree 2, you get ~5000 features — regularization is essential. In practice, only create specific interaction terms you have domain reason to believe in, rather than applying blindly.
advantages: Enables linear models to capture non-linearity
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline

# Create polynomial features up to degree 2
pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('poly', PolynomialFeatures(
        degree=2,
        interaction_only=False,  # True = only cross terms, no x²
        include_bias=False
    )),
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
    ('model', Ridge(alpha=1.0))  # MUST regularize!
])

pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f'Feature count: {X_train.shape[1]} → {pipeline["poly"].n_output_features_}')
```
